title: Inside the Luddite festival harnessing Gen Z’s rage against Big Tech
gist: A New York 'Summer of Ludd' festival channels growing Gen Z backlash against Big Tech and AI into deliberately offline, phone-free community events, reflecting rising anti-tech sentiment even as experts doubt it will change actual behavior.

themes.theme: Anti-tech backlash as a cultural trend

insights.claim: The new Luddite movement is heavily associated with Gen Z, and a 2025 Pew study found 48 percent of teens in 2024 said social media has negative effects on their peers, up from 32 percent in 2022.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: A 16-point jump in two years quantifies deteriorating sentiment among the core young demographic that social platforms monetize.
insights.quote: 48 percent of teen respondents said social media has negative effects on people their age—up from 32 percent in 2022
insights.locator: The new Luddite movement has become heavily associated

insights.claim: Anti-tech sentiment is manifesting across multiple behaviors: quitting dating apps for in-person run clubs, booing pro-AI commencement speakers, and rising popularity of analog technologies like cyberdecks.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: A cluster of leading indicators worth monitoring for downside risk to dating-app and consumer-social engagement metrics.
insights.locator: This hostility toward the outsized role of technology

insights.claim: Organizers deliberately kept all Summer of Ludd events unadvertised online, banning phones, recording, and photos, and used a puppet spokesperson ('Gowanus') to preserve organizer anonymity, treating the offline event itself as the medium for social change.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: An intentionally un-scalable, anti-viral organizing model runs directly counter to platform growth logic and may cap the movement's reach.
insights.quote: We are striving to create an event that defies consumption.
insights.locator: None of the week’s events, including the play

insights.claim: ASU professor Andrew Maynard reframes the original Luddites as a labor movement rather than an anti-tech one, casting modern Luddism as pushback against technology's erosion of personal autonomy.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Reframing anti-tech backlash as an autonomy/labor issue better predicts where organized resistance (e.g., AI-in-workplace) will surface.
insights.quote: pushing back against the prevalence of tech and how it pulls away from their autonomy on multiple fronts
insights.locator: Andrew Maynard, a professor of advanced technology

themes.theme: AI in the workplace as flashpoint

insights.claim: A former Big Tech security engineer says he quit after leadership pushed non-technical staff to write code with AI-assisted tools and ship it to production, framing AI-coding governance as a security risk driving worker disaffection.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Signals internal-employee resistance to AI-coding mandates, a governance/quality risk for firms aggressively deploying AI dev tools.
insights.quote: our leadership was encouraging non-technical people to write code with AI-assisted tools and pushing them to production
insights.locator: An attendee and former Big Tech employee

insights.claim: Unplatform founder Damian Thomas argues modern tech workers, like historical Luddites who had to rent big machines, now depend on rented infrastructure such as Claude Code and SaaS, framing platform dependence as the core grievance.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The 'rented infrastructure' framing points at where value and control accrue: to platform owners, not tool users.
insights.quote: With things like Claude Code and SaaS, that’s what we are seeing now
insights.locator: Damian Thomas, a web developer who runs Unplatform

insights.claim: Maynard doubts the movement will move the needle substantially, noting that even people who agree technologies are harmful rarely change how they live, still using phones, social media, and AI.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The attitude-behavior gap means bearish sentiment surveys may not translate into actual disengagement or revenue impact for platforms.
insights.quote: Even when people agree that they think these technologies are harmful, it rarely impacts the way they live their lives.
insights.locator: But, despite the high hopes at the Summer of Ludd

insights.claim: A former Big Tech attendee notes network effects stymie exit: leaving Facebook while friends stay cuts you off from your circle, so the gravitational pull of big platforms is likely to blunt real progress away from these tools.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Network-effect lock-in is the structural moat protecting incumbent platforms against sentiment-driven churn.
insights.quote: If you leave Facebook but all your friends are still on Facebook, you’ve just cut yourself off from your friend circle
insights.locator: Having worked in tech, the attendee says
